revision.text: #redirect[[en:Machishū]] [[Category:Automatic wikipedia redirects]]
'''町衆'''とは、[[室町時代]]～[[戦国時代]]にかけての、特に[[京都]]の裕福な商工業者である。京都復興においての重要な人物である。自治と団結を進め、文化を作った。

revision.comment: Auto-generated page using script written by Immanuelle, either as a placeholder redirect or to make bots recognize the page as made by Immanuelle

revision.text:
'''町衆'''（まちしゅう、ちょうしゅう）とは、[[室町時代]]から[[戦国時代 (日本)|戦国時代]]にかけての、特に[土倉]などの[[京都]]の裕福な商工業者である。京都復興においての重要な人物である。自治と団結を進め、文化を作った。主に法華経を信仰する。
[[平安時代]]の[[京童]]などを継承する概念で近世、[[江戸時代]]の[[町民]]へと継承される。
== 関連項目 == [[天文法華の乱]]
{{history-stub}}
[[Category:日本史|まちしゆう]] [[Category:室町時代|まちしゆう]]

[[平安時代]]の[[京童]]などを継承する概念で近世、[[江戸時代]]の[[町人]]へと継承される。

[[平安時代]]の[[京童]]などを継承する概念で近世、[[江戸時代]]の[[町人]]へと継承される。
今日では「まちしゅう」と読まれることが多いが、『[[節要集]]』『[[日葡辞書]]』などの同時代史料によれば「ちょうしゅう」と発音されていた。

revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=July 2017}} '''Machishū''' (町衆) refers to the wealthy merchants and craftsmen of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} from the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} to the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代}}, particularly those involved with {{ill|Dozō|ja|土倉}}. They were an important social class in the reconstruction of Kyoto after the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}. They promoted self-governance, unity, and the development of culture, primarily practicing faith in the {{ill|Lotus Sutra|ja|法華経}}. Their central temple was {{ill|Honnō-ji|ja|本能寺}}.
The concept of ''machishū'' is a continuation of earlier urban groups such as the {{ill|Kyowarawa|ja|京童}} of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} and later evolved into the {{ill|Chōnin|ja|町人}} class during the early modern {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
Today, it is commonly pronounced as "machishū," but contemporary historical sources such as the {{ill|Setsuyōshū|ja|節要集}} and the {{ill|Nippo Jisho|ja|日葡辞書}} suggest that it was pronounced as "chōshū."
== See Also == * [[:en:Hokke-ikki|Hokke-ikki]] * {{ill|Kan'ei culture|ja|寛永文化}} * {{ill|Machikōji|ja|町小路}}
{{Normdaten}}
[[Category:Muromachi period]] [[Category:Urban communities]]

revision.text:
{{Cite sources|date=July 2017}} '''Machishū''' (町衆) refers to the wealthy merchants and craftsmen of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都}} from the {{ill|Muromachi period|ja|室町時代}} to the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代}}, particularly those involved with {{ill|Dozō|ja|土倉}}. They were an important social class in the reconstruction of Kyoto after the {{ill|Ōnin War|ja|応仁の乱}}. They promoted self-governance, unity, and the development of culture, primarily practicing faith in the {{ill|Lotus Sutra|ja|法華経}}. Their central temple was {{ill|Honnō-ji|ja|本能寺}}.
The concept of ''machishū'' is a continuation of earlier urban groups such as the {{ill|Kyowarawa|ja|京童}} of the {{ill|Heian period|ja|平安時代}} and later evolved into the {{ill|Chōnin|ja|町人}} class during the early modern {{ill|Edo period|ja|江戸時代}}.
Today, it is commonly pronounced as "machishū," but contemporary historical sources such as the {{ill|Setsuyōshū|ja|節要集}} and the {{ill|Nippo Jisho|ja|日葡辞書}} suggest that it was pronounced as "chōshū."
== See Also == * [[:en:Hokke-ikki|Hokke-ikki]] * {{ill|Kan'ei culture|ja|寛永文化}} * {{ill|Machikōji|ja|町小路}}
{{Normdaten}}

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 07:56, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}}
{{Unreferenced|date=July 2017}}
'''Machishū''' (町衆) refers to the wealthy merchants and craftsmen of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|qq=|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} from the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] to the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period (Japan)|qq=|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, particularly those involved with {{ill|Dozō|ja|土倉|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dozō|qq=|1=dosō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423079}}. They were an important social class in the reconstruction of Kyoto after the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]]. They promoted self-governance, unity, and the development of culture, primarily practicing faith in the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]]. Their central temple was [[:en:Honnō-ji|Honnō-ji]].

The concept of ''machishū'' is a continuation of earlier urban groups such as the {{ill|Kyowarawa|ja|京童|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyowarawa|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and later evolved into the [[:en:Chōnin|Chōnin]] class during the early modern [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].

Today, it is commonly pronounced as "machishū," but contemporary historical sources such as the {{ill|Setsuyōshū|ja|節要集|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Setsuyōshū|qq=|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the [[:en:Nippo Jisho|Nippo Jisho]] suggest that it was pronounced as "chōshū."

'''Machishū''' (町衆) refers to the wealthy merchants and craftsmen of {{ill|Kyoto|ja|京都|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyoto|1=Kyōto|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q740246}} from the [[:en:Muromachi period|Muromachi period]] to the {{ill|Sengoku period (Japan)|ja|戦国時代|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Sengoku period (Japan)|1=Sengoku Period (Japan)|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q117415390}}, particularly those involved with {{ill|Dozō|ja|土倉|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Dozō|1=dosō|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11423079}}. They were an important social class in the reconstruction of Kyoto after the [[:en:Ōnin War|Ōnin War]]. They promoted self-governance, unity, and the development of culture, primarily practicing faith in the [[:en:Lotus Sutra|Lotus Sutra]]. Their central temple was [[:en:Honnō-ji|Honnō-ji]].

The concept of ''machishū'' is a continuation of earlier urban groups such as the {{ill|Kyowarawa|ja|京童|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyowarawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the [[:en:Heian period|Heian period]] and later evolved into the [[:en:Chōnin|Chōnin]] class during the early modern [[:en:Edo period|Edo period]].

They were an important social class in the reconstruction of Kyoto after the {{ill|Ōnin War|en|Ōnin War|WD=Q385676|lt=Ōnin War}}. They promoted self-governance, unity, and the development of culture, primarily practicing faith in the {{ill|Lotus Sutra|en|Lotus Sutra|WD=Q861212|lt=Lotus Sutra}}. Their central temple was {{ill|Honnō-ji|en|Honnō-ji|WD=Q626535|lt=Honnō-ji}}.

The concept of ''machishū'' is a continuation of earlier urban groups such as the {{ill|Kyowarawa|ja|京童|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Kyowarawa|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} of the {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}} and later evolved into the {{ill|Chōnin|en|Chōnin|WD=Q115847|lt=Chōnin}} class during the early modern {{ill|Edo period|en|Edo period|WD=Q184963|lt=Edo period}}.

Today, it is commonly pronounced as "machishū," but contemporary historical sources such as the {{ill|Setsuyōshū|ja|節要集|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Setsuyōshū|ja_comment=jawiki link invalid}} and the {{ill|Nippo Jisho|en|Nippo Jisho|WD=Q723637|lt=Nippo Jisho}} suggest that it was pronounced as "chōshū."
